model_narrative: We schedule batch production over a discrete time horizon on a network of processing units that transform materials held in storage states. For every time period we decide which task to start in which unit and how big its batch should be, and we track how much material sits in each storage state and each unit over time. The aim is to maximize the value of the final product inventories net of the total production cost.

params.name: rho
params.doc: input fraction: the share of a task's batch that is drawn from a feeding state when the task runs; the keys of this parameter are exactly the valid task-to-feeding-state input arcs

params.name: rho_
params.doc: output fraction: the share of a task's batch that is delivered to a receiving state when the task completes; the keys of this parameter are exactly the valid task-to-receiving-state output arcs

params.name: C
params.doc: maximum storage capacity of each state, in material units; the amount of that material that can be held at any time cannot exceed this

params.name: Bmax
params.doc: maximum batch size for a task when run in a given unit, in material units; the keys of this parameter are exactly the valid task-unit pairs, i.e. which units are capable of which tasks

params.name: Bmin
params.doc: minimum batch size for a task when run in a given unit, in material units; only meaningful when that task is actually started in that unit

params.name: Pi
params.doc: external material entering (positive) or leaving (negative) a state at a time period, in material units

vars.name: W
vars.doc: binary start indicator: 1 if the task is started in the unit at the time period, 0 otherwise; defined only over valid task-unit pairs and all time periods

vars.name: B
vars.doc: size of the batch assigned to the task in the unit at the time period, in material units; defined over the same task-unit-time index as the start indicator

vars.name: S
vars.doc: inventory level of each state at each time period, in material units

vars.name: Q
vars.doc: inventory held in each unit at each time period, in material units

vars.name: Value
vars.doc: total revenue from product inventories at the end of the planning horizon, in dollars

vars.name: Cost
vars.doc: total production cost over the horizon, in dollars

constraints.name: S[s,t]
constraints.intent: Each storage state has a fixed holding capacity. For every state and every time period, the amount of material held in that state cannot exceed the capacity of that state.

constraints.name: Bmin[i,j]*
constraints.intent: A batch may only be assigned to a task in a unit if that task is actually started in that unit at that time, and when it is started the batch cannot be too small. For every valid task and unit and every time period, the batch size must be at least the minimum batch size whenever the task is started in that unit, and must be zero when it is not started.

constraints.name: B[i,j,t]
constraints.intent: When a task is started in a unit at a time period, the batch assigned to it cannot exceed that unit's maximum batch size for the task, and if the task is not started in that unit then no batch may be assigned. For every valid task and unit and every time period, the batch size is bounded above by the maximum batch size when the task is started there and forced to zero otherwise.

query: Procurement is bracing for a feedstock squeeze next quarter — they want us to finish this horizon holding at least 1250 units of raw material across Feed_A, Feed_B and Feed_C combined. What does the optimizer return for net value if we bank that reserve?

query: Maintenance is pitching a wear-leveling policy: no single piece of equipment — the heater, either reactor, or the still — should fire more than five batch starts over the whole horizon. What net value does the optimizer come back with under that per-unit cap?

query: There's a push to reduce changeovers on the floor — limit the total number of task starts across the schedule to 14. What's the resulting net value?
expected_pyomo: model.start_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.W[k] for k in model.W) <= 14)

query: We're planning a utilities overhaul window, and both restrictions land in the same horizon: the Heater can be fired at most once, and the Still gets a single separation campaign. Run the two together — where does net value end up?
expected_pyomo:
model.heater_budget = Constraint(expr=sum(model.W[i, 'Heater', t] for (i, j, t) in model.W if j == 'Heater') <= 1)
model.still_budget = Constraint(expr=sum(model.W[i, 'Still', t] for (i, j, t) in model.W if j == 'Still') <= 1)

query: The schedule finishes the horizon with a lot more Product_2 on the shelf than Product_1, even though Product_1 is the flagship line. Shouldn't we be ending with at least as much Product_1 in stock as Product_2? What's the model seeing that makes the lopsided finish better?

query: Reaction_2 fires five separate times across the two reactors — that's a lot of churn on a single task. Couldn't three Reaction_2 runs cover it? What are those extra batches actually buying us?
expected_pyomo: model.r2_task_budget = Constraint(expr=sum(model.W['Reaction_2', j, t] for (i, j, t) in model.W if i == 'Reaction_2') <= 3)

query: All of the separation work lands in the back half of the schedule, which leaves the finishing crew slammed at the end of the run. Shouldn't at least 40% of the total separation volume the still processes over the whole horizon already be through it by period 5? What's pushing it all so late?
expected_pyomo: model.early_separation = Constraint(expr=sum(model.B['Separation', 'Still', t] for t in range(0, 6)) >= 0.4 * sum(model.B['Separation', 'Still', t] for t in range(0, 11)))

query: The schedule fires off a lot of separate task starts. Couldn't we just consolidate down to at most 13 starts across the horizon? What's the model seeing that I'm not?
expected_pyomo: model.fewer_starts = Constraint(expr=sum(model.W[k] for k in model.W) <= 13)
